g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Second and Third Normal Forms in database design, essential for creating efficient databases.
Poor database design leads to update anomalies, insertion issues, and deletion problems that corrupt data integrity.
g.text: Update Anomalies - Same data repeated, hard to maintain
Insertion Anomalies - Cannot add certain data
Deletion Anomalies - Lost data when deleting

g.data-narration: Before learning 2NF and 3NF, we must understand First Normal Form requirements and how to identify candidate keys.
Second Normal Form requires being in 1NF and having no partial dependencies where non-prime attributes depend on part of a candidate key.
g.text: 1. Must be in First Normal Form (1NF)
then A must be a prime attribute

g.data-narration: A partial dependency occurs when a non-prime attribute depends only on part of a composite candidate key, not the entire key.
Prime attributes are those appearing in any candidate key, while all other attributes are non-prime attributes that must be fully dependent.
g.text: Attributes that are part of ANY candidate key
Attributes NOT part of any candidate key

g.data-narration: In relation R(A,B,C,D) with FD AB→CD and A→C, attribute C has a partial dependency on A, violating 2NF.
g.text: A → C (C depends on part of key)

g.data-narration: Third Normal Form builds on 2NF by eliminating transitive dependencies where non-prime attributes depend on other non-prime attributes.
g.text: 1. Must be in Second Normal Form (2NF)
∀(X → A) ∈ F⁺: X is superkey OR A is prime

g.data-narration: A transitive dependency occurs when a non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute that depends on the candidate key.
In relation R(StudentID, CourseID, CourseFee, Instructor) with FD CourseID→CourseFee, CourseFee has a transitive dependency.
g.text: FDs: {StudentID, CourseID → CourseFee, Instructor; CourseID → CourseFee}

g.data-narration: 2NF eliminates partial dependencies from composite keys while 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies from any candidate key.
For R(A,B,C,D) with FDs AB→CD, A→C, B→D, we decompose into R1(A,C), R2(B,D) to eliminate partial dependencies.
For complex relations, first decompose to 2NF then check for transitive dependencies to achieve full 3NF normalization.
Remember: 2NF eliminates partial dependencies from composite keys, while 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies between non-prime attributes.
Always identify candidate keys first, understand the difference between partial and transitive dependencies, and remember sequential normalization.
